definition: In a tree, a node on the path from the root to a given node. A node's parent, grandparent, and so on are its ancestors.
A multiway balanced search tree designed to store many keys per node. B-trees are especially useful for databases and file systems because they reduce the number of disk or block accesses.
A variant of a B-tree in which all actual records or record pointers are stored at the leaves, while internal nodes guide the search. Leaf nodes are often linked for efficient range queries.
A collection that allows duplicate values and usually does not emphasize order. It is like a set except that the same value may occur more than once.
A value used in some balanced trees, commonly computed as the height of one subtree minus the height of the other. In AVL trees, it helps determine whether a rotation is needed.
A tree that keeps its height small relative to the number of nodes, usually O(log n), so that operations such as search, insertion, and deletion remain efficient.
A storage location in a hash table or bucket-based structure. A bucket may store one element, a list of elements, or another small structure.
The amount of storage currently allocated by a container, which may be larger than the number of elements it actually contains.
In a tree, a node directly below another node. If node p has a link to node c, then c is a child of p.
A linked list in which the last node points back to the first node, allowing traversal to wrap around instead of ending at null.
In hashing, the event that two different keys are assigned to the same table index or bucket by the hash function.
In a tree, the number of edges on the path from the root to a node. The root has depth 0.
A sequence data structure that allows insertion and removal at both the front and the back.
In a tree, a node below a given node. A node's children, grandchildren, and so on are its descendants.
An abstract data type that stores key-value pairs and supports operations such as inserting a pair, searching by key, updating a value, and removing a key.
An edge with a direction, going from one vertex to another. Directed edges are used in directed graphs.
A graph whose edges have direction. An edge from u to v does not necessarily imply an edge from v to u.
A collection of sets that do not overlap. The disjoint-set ADT usually supports making singleton sets, finding which set contains an element, and unioning two sets.
A linked list in which each node stores links to both the next node and the previous node, allowing efficient movement in both directions.
An array-backed list that grows by allocating a larger array and copying elements when capacity is exhausted. Appending is usually O(1) amortized time.
A connection between two vertices in a graph. Edges may be directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted.
A node with no children, often also called a leaf. In some tree descriptions, external nodes may refer to special null placeholders used to simplify algorithms.
In the disjoint-set ADT, the operation that returns a representative for the set containing a given element.
The ordering rule for a heap. In a min-heap, each node's key is no larger than its children's keys; in a max-heap, each node's key is no smaller than its children's keys.
For a node in a tree, the number of edges on the longest downward path from that node to a leaf. The height of a tree is the height of its root.
In a directed graph, the number of edges entering a vertex.
A binary tree traversal that visits the left subtree, then the node, then the right subtree. For a binary search tree, inorder traversal visits keys in sorted order.
A tree node that has at least one child. This contrasts with a leaf, which has no children.
An object or mechanism that visits the elements of a collection one at a time without requiring the user to know the collection's internal representation.
A value used to identify, search for, or order an entry in a data structure such as a map, search tree, heap, or hash table.
A node in a tree with no children.
All tree nodes at the same depth. The root is on level 0 if depth is counted by edges.
A tree traversal that visits nodes by depth, usually from left to right within each level. It is typically implemented with a queue.
A heap in which every parent key is greater than or equal to its children's keys, so the maximum key is stored at the root.
A heap in which every parent key is less than or equal to its children's keys, so the minimum key is stored at the root.
A basic unit in a linked structure, usually storing data plus one or more references or pointers to related nodes.
A hash table collision strategy in which all entries are stored directly in the table array. When a collision occurs, the algorithm probes other positions.
In a directed graph, the number of edges leaving a vertex.
In a tree, the node directly above another node. Every node except the root has exactly one parent.
A sequence of vertices connected by edges. In a tree, there is exactly one simple path between any two nodes.
A value that refers to another object or memory location. Linked data structures use pointers or references to connect nodes.
A tree traversal that visits each child subtree before visiting the node itself. For a binary tree, this means left subtree, right subtree, then node.
A tree traversal that visits the node before its child subtrees. For a binary tree, this means node, left subtree, then right subtree.
In an open-addressed hash table, the sequence of table positions examined while searching for a key or finding a place to insert it.
The process of rebuilding a hash table, usually with a larger capacity, and reinserting existing keys using the hash function for the new table size.
The top node of a tree. In a rooted tree, every other node is a descendant of the root.
A local restructuring operation in a binary search tree that changes parent-child relationships while preserving sorted order. Rotations are used by balanced trees.
A special node used to simplify boundary cases in a linked structure, such as insertions or deletions at the front or back of a list.
An abstract data type that stores distinct elements and supports operations such as add, remove, contains, union, intersection, and difference.
In a tree, nodes that have the same parent.
A linked list in which each node stores a link to the next node, but not to the previous node.
The number of elements currently stored in a data structure.
A graph with relatively few edges compared with the maximum possible number of edges. Sparse graphs are often represented with adjacency lists.
A tree formed by choosing a node and including all of its descendants.
In a binary search tree, the node with the smallest key greater than a given node's key. It is often used when deleting a node with two children.
A marker left behind after deletion in some open-addressed hash tables. It indicates that a slot is no longer occupied but that probing should continue past it.
A tree used to store strings by sharing common prefixes. Tries support operations such as prefix search and dictionary lookup.
In the disjoint-set ADT, the operation that combines two sets into one set.
An object in a graph. Vertices are connected by edges.
